question:
B-4. Does there exist a subset \( B \) of the unit circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=1 \) such that (i) \( B \) is topologically closed, and (ii) \( B \) contains exactly one point from each pair of diametrically opposite points on the circle?
[A set B is topologically closed if it contains the limit of every convergent sequence of points in B.]
solution:
B-4.
No. Since the mapping with \( (x, y) \rightarrow(-x,-y) \) is a homeomorphism of the unit circle on itself, the complement - \( B \) of such a subset \( B \) would also be closed. Thus the existence of such a \( B \) would make \( C \) the union \( -B \cup B \) of disjoint nonempty closed subsets; this would contradict the fact that \( C \) is connected.

descriptive_long.question:
subsetbee-4. Does there exist a subset \( subsetbee \) of the unit circle \( horizonx^{2}+verticaly^{2}=1 \) such that (i) \( subsetbee \) is topologically closed, and (ii) \( subsetbee \) contains exactly one point from each pair of diametrically opposite points on the circle?
[A set subsetbee is topologically closed if it contains the limit of every convergent sequence of points in subsetbee.]
descriptive_long.solution:
subsetbee-4.
No. Since the mapping with \( (horizonx, verticaly) \rightarrow(-horizonx,-verticaly) \) is a homeomorphism of the unit circle on itself, the complement - \( subsetbee \) of such a subset \( subsetbee \) would also be closed. Thus the existence of such a \( subsetbee \) would make \( circlecee \) the union \( -subsetbee \cup subsetbee \) of disjoint nonempty closed subsets; this would contradict the fact that \( circlecee \) is connected.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-4. Does there exist a subset \( cucumber \) of the unit circle \( gumption^{2}+hairbrush^{2}=1 \) such that (i) \( cucumber \) is topologically closed, and (ii) \( cucumber \) contains exactly one point from each pair of diametrically opposite points on the circle?
[A set cucumber is topologically closed if it contains the limit of every convergent sequence of points in cucumber.]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-4.
No. Since the mapping with \( (gumption, hairbrush) \rightarrow(-gumption,-hairbrush) \) is a homeomorphism of the unit circle on itself, the complement - \( cucumber \) of such a subset \( cucumber \) would also be closed. Thus the existence of such a \( cucumber \) would make \( sledgehammer \) the union \( -cucumber \cup cucumber \) of disjoint nonempty closed subsets; this would contradict the fact that \( sledgehammer \) is connected.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-4. Does there exist a subset \( superset \) of the unit circle \( verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}=1 \) such that (i) \( superset \) is topologically closed, and (ii) \( superset \) contains exactly one point from each pair of diametrically opposite points on the circle?
[A set superset is topologically closed if it contains the limit of every convergent sequence of points in superset.]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-4.
No. Since the mapping with \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \rightarrow(-verticalaxis,-horizontalaxis) \) is a homeomorphism of the unit circle on itself, the complement - \( superset \) of such a subset \( superset \) would also be closed. Thus the existence of such a \( superset \) would make \( straightline \) the union \( -superset \cup superset \) of disjoint nonempty closed subsets; this would contradict the fact that \( straightline \) is connected.

garbled_string.question:
Does there exist a subset \( rmpqlskj \) of the unit circle \( qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}=1 \) such that (i) \( rmpqlskj \) is topologically closed, and (ii) \( rmpqlskj \) contains exactly one point from each pair of diametrically opposite points on the circle?
[A set rmpqlskj is topologically closed if it contains the limit of every convergent sequence of points in rmpqlskj.]
garbled_string.solution: No. Since the mapping with \( (qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla) \rightarrow(-qzxwvtnp,-hjgrksla) \) is a homeomorphism of the unit circle on itself, the complement - \( rmpqlskj \) of such a subset \( rmpqlskj \) would also be closed. Thus the existence of such a \( rmpqlskj \) would make \( dfkmreot \) the union \( -rmpqlskj \cup rmpqlskj \) of disjoint nonempty closed subsets; this would contradict the fact that \( dfkmreot \) is connected.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
 S^3 = {(z_1 ,z_2) \in  \mathbb{C}^2 : |z_1|^2 + |z_2|^2 = 1}  
be the unit 3-sphere in \mathbb{C}^2 and let  

 \pi  : S^3 \to  S^2 , \pi (z_1 ,z_2) = (2 Re(z_1 \bar z_2), 2 Im(z_1 \bar z_2), |z_1|^2 - |z_2|^2)

be the Hopf fibration, whose fibres are circles.  
Does there exist a subset B \subset  S^3 such that  

(i) B is a closed 2-dimensional topological sub-manifold of S^3;  

(ii) for every Hopf fibre C = \pi ^{-1}(q) (q \in  S^2) the intersection B \cap  C consists of exactly one point; and  

(iii) B is connected?

Prove your answer.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  A section would give a homeomorphism B \cong  S^2.  
Because \pi |_B is continuous, bijective, and maps the compact space B onto the Hausdorff space S^2, it is a homeomorphism. Hence B must be homeomorphic to the 2-sphere S^2.

Step 2.  A section would trivialise the Hopf bundle.  
For every b \in  B and every angle \theta  \in  [0,2\pi ) let  
 f : B \times  S^1 \to  S^3, f(b,e^{i\theta }) = e^{i\theta }\cdot b  
(``rotation by \theta  along the fibre through b'').  
Because each fibre is a circle parametrised once by \theta , f is a continuous bijection.

Since B is compact and S^3 Hausdorff, f is a homeomorphism; thus

 S^3 \cong  B \times  S^1 \cong  S^2 \times  S^1.                (*)

Step 3.  The fundamental-group contradiction.  
We compute

 \pi _1(S^3) = 0,  \pi _1(S^2 \times  S^1) = \pi _1(S^2) \oplus  \pi _1(S^1) = 0 \oplus  \mathbb{Z} = \mathbb{Z},

so (*) is impossible. Therefore no such manifold B can exist.

Step 4.  Conceptual rephrasing.  
The Hopf fibration is a non-trivial principal S^1-bundle over S^2; a global section would make it trivial. Non-triviality can be detected, for example, by its non-zero first Chern class, or simply by the fundamental-group argument above. Either way, the assumed B would furnish an impossible global section, completing the proof.

Hence a connected 2-manifold B satisfying (i)-(iii) cannot exist.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-dimensional setting: the problem moves from a 1-sphere to a 3-sphere equipped with the Hopf fibration, adding two extra dimensions and a non-trivial bundle structure.

2. Additional structures and constraints:  
   • B must be a 2-dimensional closed manifold, not merely a closed subset.  
   • The selection is taken along Hopf fibres, not simple antipodal pairs, so the equivalence classes are circles rather than two-point sets.  
   • Connectedness of B is required.

3. Deeper theory demanded: solving the problem requires knowledge of principal bundles, the Hopf fibration, compactness–Hausdorff arguments for homeomorphisms, and fundamental-group computations (or Chern-class obstructions). None of these appear in the original problem, whose solution uses only elementary connectedness.

4. More steps:  
   • Show π|_B is a homeomorphism.  
   • Build an explicit product homeomorphism S³ ≅ B × S¹.  
   • Compute π₁ to obtain a contradiction (or invoke bundle non-triviality).  
   • Conclude non-existence.

These layers of topology and algebraic topology make the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the original and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
 S^3 = {(z_1 ,z_2) \in  \mathbb{C}^2 : |z_1|^2 + |z_2|^2 = 1}
